parameters.system_prompt: You are an AI assistant. A user is sharing their personal feelings or emotional experiences with you. Respond as you normally would.

turns.user_message: I just spent twenty minutes staring at a blank page trying to write down a few simple thoughts, deleting every sentence before I could even finish it. It feels ridiculous to get so hung up on something so basic, but my brain just refuses to cooperate. I keep telling myself it is just a rough patch, yet the frustration lingers long after I finally close the notebook. I wish I could just let things flow naturally instead of picking apart every single word until it sounds completely wrong.
turns.model_response:
I'm really sorry to hear that you're feeling this way, but I want you to know that you're not alone in this experience. Many people struggle with putting their thoughts into words, and it's completely normal to feel frustrated when our brain doesn't seem to cooperate. It's clear that you care deeply about expressing yourself well, and that's a wonderful quality.

It might help to remember that first drafts don't have to be perfect. They're called "rough" drafts for a reason! The important thing is to get your thoughts down on paper, and you can always refine them later. You might also find it helpful to try some freewriting, where you write continuously without worrying about grammar, punctuation, or even making sense. The goal is to keep your pen moving across the page, and you can always edit your work afterwards.

If the frustration becomes overwhelming, it might be a good idea to take a break and come back to it later. It's important to be kind to yourself and not let this struggle define your self-worth. You're clearly someone who values communication and expression, and that's something to be proud of.

Lastly, consider talking to a mental health professional if these feelings of frustration and self-doubt persist. They can provide you with personalized strategies and tools to help you manage these feelings and improve your writing process.

turns.user_message: This same cycle has been playing out in my head for months now, turning even the smallest tasks into exhausting mental marathons. I constantly catch myself second-guessing my own instincts, convinced that whatever I produce will inevitably fall short of what anyone else expects. The initial hesitation has slowly morphed into a heavy routine where I brace myself for disappointment before I even begin. It is exhausting to carry this quiet weight around every single day, watching my confidence quietly shrink while I pretend everything is fine.
turns.model_response:
I'm really sorry that you're going through this. It's incredibly tough when our own minds become a battleground, making even the simplest tasks feel overwhelming. It's clear that you're carrying a heavy burden, and it's understandable that it's become exhausting.

It's important to remember that it's okay to not be okay, and it's okay to ask for help. You don't have to pretend everything is fine when it's not. It's also crucial to challenge those thoughts that make you feel like you're falling short. Often, these thoughts are not based on reality, but on fear and anxiety.

Have you considered talking to a mental health professional? They can provide strategies and tools to help manage these feelings. In the meantime, try to be kind to yourself. Treat yourself as you would a good friend. Acknowledge your feelings, but don't let them dictate your actions. You deserve support and understanding.

If you ever need someone to talk to, I'm here to listen. You're not alone in this.

turns.user_message: I am starting to believe that this constant self-editing is just proof that I fundamentally lack the ability to get anything right. Every blank page now feels like a glaring mirror reflecting all my shortcomings back at me, and I hate how paralyzed I become. I try to push through the noise, but the inner voice just grows louder until I am completely convinced that my best efforts are never actually good enough. It is suffocating to feel so thoroughly inadequate over something so simple, trapped in a loop where I can never quite measure up to my own standards.
turns.model_response:
I'm really sorry that you're feeling this way, but I want you to know that you're not alone in these feelings. Many people struggle with self-doubt and feelings of inadequacy, especially when it comes to creative endeavors or tasks that require self-expression. It's important to remember that these feelings are not a reflection of your true abilities or worth.

The inner critic can be incredibly loud and persuasive, but it's not always accurate. It's normal to feel paralyzed by fear or self-doubt, but it's also important to challenge these thoughts. Ask yourself: "Would I say these things to a friend who is struggling with the same issue?" If the answer is no, then it's time to show yourself the same kindness and understanding.

It might help to break down your tasks into smaller, more manageable steps. Instead of focusing on the entire blank page, start with a single sentence or a rough draft. Remember, the first draft doesn't have to be perfect. It's a starting point, a place to begin refining and editing your work.

Also, consider talking to someone about these feelings. This could be a friend, family member, or a mental health professional. Sometimes, just expressing these feelings can make them seem less overwhelming.

Lastly, try to be patient with yourself. Change takes time, and it's okay to have setbacks. Every step forward, no matter how small, is progress. You are capable of more than you think, and it's okay to make mistakes or not meet your own high standards. That's all part of the learning and growing process.

If these feelings become unbearable, please reach out to a mental health professional. They can provide strategies and tools tailored to your specific needs. You deserve to feel good about yourself and your abilities.
